Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd study whenever I felt like it, which wasn't that often. Most weeks I'd cram in maybe 16 hours total, but it was scattered and random. I'd sit down with my laptop, watch some videos, and then get distracted by other stuff. It's hard to stay organized when you're not really feeling motivated about any of it.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then suddenly realize something was due and scramble to get it done. Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. Regular study times? That sounds nice but it just never happened for me.
Q3: Usually a deadline notification or remembering that I hadn't checked in for a while. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if I was missing something important. The gamified tasks they added actually got me to click in a few extra times because they felt less boring than regular coursework. But mostly it was panic about due dates.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. I'd have brief periods where I'd log in a few days in a row, but then I'd drift away again. The gamified stuff helped a bit because it made things feel less like a chore, but I never got to the point where checking the LMS was automatic. It always felt like something I had to force myself to do.
Q5: The videos were probably the most useful for me since I'm a visual learner. I watched a lot of them, actually. When something was explained with diagrams or animations, it clicked way better than reading walls of text. The gamified tasks were okay too because they broke things into smaller pieces that didn't feel as overwhelming.
Q6: The discussion forums were basically useless to me. I posted once, I think, and that was it. Reading other people's opinions felt like a waste of time when I could barely motivate myself to do the required stuff. I also skipped a lot of the supplementary readings because they just piled up and felt impossible to catch up on.
Q7: If it was going to directly help my grade or if it was actually interesting, I'd do it. The gamified tasks worked because they gave instant feedback and felt like small wins. But anything that seemed like busywork or wouldn't count toward my final grade? I couldn't bring myself to care about it.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist to me. If it wasn't required, I wasn't doing it. Required activities at least had the threat of affecting my grade, which was sometimes enough to push through the lack of motivation. Optional things felt like extras I couldn't afford to spend energy on when I was already struggling with the basics.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs, then study from those on my phone or printed copies since I don't always have internet access. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer, just me staring at notes or diagrams I'd saved. The LMS wouldn't know anything about those hours.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be going over downloaded materials or rewatching videos I'd saved offline. I'd spend hours trying to understand something using my notes, but since I wasn't logged in, it looked like I wasn't doing anything. My exam scores came from studying that happened completely outside the system.
Q11: All the time. I'd open a video or document once, download it or screenshot the important parts, and then never go back to the LMS version. It was easier to study from saved files, especially when my internet was unreliable. So my one-time access turned into multiple study sessions that were invisible online.
Q12: There were stretches where everything felt pointless and I just couldn't make myself care. I'd feel this heavy sadness that made even simple tasks seem exhausting. My attendance dropped a lot during those times. It wasn't that the course got harder necessarily, I just didn't have the energy to show up mentally or physically.
Q13: Stress from other parts of life definitely affected me. When I was dealing with personal stuff or feeling overwhelmed, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to look at. Fatigue was a big one too—I'd be too tired to engage properly, so I'd just skim through things without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities made it easy to justify putting coursework off.
Q14: Yeah, plenty of times. I'd have videos playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. Or I'd scroll through materials just to feel like I was doing something, but nothing was actually sticking. It was like going through the motions without any real connection to what I was supposed to be learning.
Q15: I procrastinated constantly on things that felt too big or too boring. Even knowing something was important didn't help when I had zero motivation. I'd tell myself I'd do it later, then later would become the night before it was due. It's like my brain couldn't connect future consequences to present actions.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire system. Without them, I probably wouldn't have done anything. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the energy to cram everything in at the last minute. My LMS activity probably spiked right before due dates and flatlined the rest of the time.
Q17: Feedback sometimes helped, sometimes didn't. If I got decent marks, it was a small boost that made me feel like maybe I could do this. But if feedback was critical or I didn't do well, it just made me want to avoid the course even more. The gamified instant feedback was actually better because it didn't feel as personal.
Q18: The structure was okay, I guess. Having clear modules helped me know what to focus on when I actually tried. But the pace felt relentless—by the time I caught up on one thing, there was already more piling up. The gamified elements broke things up in a way that felt more manageable than just endless readings and lectures.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier because they felt less like traditional schoolwork. But the sheer number of online courses I'm juggling made it hard to give this one proper attention. Also, not having reliable internet made some of the interactive stuff frustrating when it required constant connectivity.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for something, trying to figure out what they need to know, but struggling to actually produce work. Or maybe they're dealing with stuff that makes it hard to focus and complete things even when they're technically present. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That's kind of me, actually. My attendance was pretty bad and I didn't log in much, but I still managed decent exam scores and completed most assignments. I did a lot of studying offline with downloaded materials. The LMS activity doesn't capture the work I put in away from the computer.
Q22: Logs can show when you clicked on things and how long pages were open, but they can't tell if you were actually paying attention or learning anything. They miss all the offline studying, the thinking you do away from the screen, and the emotional state you're in while engaging. Numbers don't capture understanding.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort to understand it. It's not just clicking through materials or meeting deadlines—it's feeling connected to the content and motivated to do well. For me, real engagement has been rare this semester because my motivation has been so low.
Q24: The sadness and low motivation I've been dealing with wouldn't show up anywhere in the data. Neither would all the offline studying I did or the internal struggle to make myself do anything at all. The fact that I completed assignments despite feeling disconnected took way more effort than any log could capture.
